source.title: Hydrogen Sulfide Release at Aghorn Operating Waterflood Station

actions_taken: Pumper A closed pump discharge valve and partially closed suction valve
event.summary: On October 26, 2019, Pumper A responded to a pump oil level alarm at Aghorn's Foster D waterflood station. While attempting to isolate Pump #1 without performing Lockout/Tagout, the pump automatically energized, causing a broken plunger to release water containing hydrogen sulfide gas. Pumper A was fatally injured from H2S exposure. Subsequently, Pumper A's spouse gained access to the unlocked facility, was also exposed to H2S, and was fatally injured. The incident was attributed to multiple safety failures including nonuse of personal H2S detectors, lack of Lockout/Tagout procedures, inadequate ventilation, nonfunctioning H2S detection and alarm systems, and deficient site security.
recommendations: Mandate use of personal H2S detection devices as integral part of PPE kit for all employees and visitors prior to entering facilities with H2S potential
Develop site-specific, formalized, comprehensive Lockout/Tagout program meeting 29 CFR 1910.147 requirements
Commission independent analysis of facility design regarding ventilation and mitigation systems for H2S release scenarios
Develop and demonstrate comprehensive safety management program addressing H2S hazards including risk identification, assessment, mitigation, and monitoring
Ensure H2S detection and alarm systems are properly maintained, configured, and include site-specific programs based on manufacturer specifications
Employ multiple layers of alerts (audible and visual) in H2S detection and alarm system design
Develop and implement formal, written, site-specific security program requiring employees to lock access gates upon entering and departing facility
key_phrases: Confinement of H2S Inside Pump House
Nonfunctioning H2S Detection and Alarm System

threats.name: Failure to perform Lockout/Tagout before pump maintenance
Pump automatic energization during isolation attempt
Broken pump plunger causing loss of containment
Inadequate ventilation of H2S gas from pump house
Nonfunctioning H2S detection and alarm system
Nonuse of personal H2S detector by worker
Unauthorized access to facility by non-employee

consequences.name: Fatal H2S exposure to Pumper A
Fatal H2S exposure to Pumper A's spouse
High H2S concentrations in pump house

controls.name: Personal H2S Detection Device
controls.barrier_role: Detection of H2S presence to alert worker
controls.lod_basis: Personal protective equipment to detect hazardous gas exposure

supporting_text: Pumper A was not wearing his personal H2S detection device upon entering the waterflood station on the night of the incident
there is no evidence that Aghorn management required the use of these devices

controls.name: Lockout/Tagout Procedure
controls.barrier_role: Isolation of energized equipment before maintenance work
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control to prevent energization of equipment during maintenance

supporting_text: At the time of the incident, Aghorn did not have any written Lockout / Tagout policies or procedures
Pumper A did not perform Lockout / Tagout to deenergize Pump #1 before performing work on it
The automatic activation of the pump allowed water containing H2S to release from the pump

controls.name: Pump House Ventilation System
controls.barrier_role: Removal of H2S gas from enclosed space
controls.lod_basis: Engineering control to mitigate consequences of H2S release

supporting_text: The pump house could be ventilated by two bay doors on the east side of the pump house, exhaust fans on the west wall opposite of the bay doors, and natural vents on each of the four outside walls
Due to the limitations of the available evidence, the CSB was unable to confirm whether the exhaust fans were operational at the time of the incident
On the night of the incident, the bay doors were approximately 60% open
The available ventilation methods did not adequately ventilate toxic H2S gas from the building during the incident

controls.name: H2S Detection and Alarm System
controls.barrier_role: Detection and alerting of H2S release to enable evacuation
controls.lod_basis: Engineering control to detect and alarm H2S release

supporting_text: The pump house was equipped with an H2S detection and alarm system
the H2S control panel did not receive signals from the internal and external detection sensors at the facility
did not trigger either of the two H2S alarms on the night of the incident

controls.name: Site Security and Access Control
controls.barrier_role: Prevention of unauthorized access to H2S hazard area
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control to prevent exposure of non-employees to H2S hazard

supporting_text: As per Aghorn's informal policy, when an Aghorn employee is working at the facility, the access gates are normally left unlocked
The unlocked gates allowed Pumper A's spouse to drive directly to the waterflood station and enter the pump house, where she was exposed to toxic H2S gas

controls.name: Safety Management Program
controls.barrier_role: Organizational framework for identifying and managing H2S hazards
controls.lod_basis: Administrative control establishing safety policies and procedures

supporting_text: The CSB requested from Aghorn 'all written policies and procedures used by Aghorn Operating.' Aghorn's response included: 1) a cell phone use policy, 2) an alarm call out procedure, 3) a Lockout / Tagout policy and procedure that was created post-incident, and 4) a pamphlet on H2S hazards
Aghorn had no additional formal company safety or operational policies or procedures

notes.rules: JSON output only. mentioned fields must be evidence-based. Use null for unknown values.
